title: One more video
body:
Mia is watching something she doesn't even like anymore. She noticed that about four videos ago, but the next one started before she could decide anything.

She sets her phone face-down on the mattress. Not dramatically. Not as a big decision. Just... face-down.

The room is quiet in a way it wasn't a second ago. She can hear the building settle. Her own breathing. Her body is heavier than she realised — she didn't notice how tired she was until she stopped reaching for the next thing.

She doesn't know if she'll fall asleep quickly. But she's stopped adding to the problem, and that feels like enough.

What are you actually looking for right now — entertainment, comfort, or just something to fill the space?

title: The version of tomorrow
body:
Rohan knows exactly what tomorrow morning feels like when he's done this. The alarm is a small violence. Everything takes longer. He moves through the first hour of the day like he's underwater.

He's not making a grand resolution tonight. He's just tired of gifting his future self that feeling.

He puts the phone on charge across the room — not because he's disciplined, but because it's the smallest possible thing he can do. He doesn't have to be asleep in ten minutes. He just has to stop the scroll.

He lies back. The ceiling is dark. His body starts to remember what rest is.

What would make tomorrow morning 10% easier — and is there one small thing you could do right now to make that more likely?

title: Reclaiming the night
body:
Priya's day belonged to everyone else. Lectures, the group project, the flatmate who needed to talk, the reading she's behind on. By 11pm she's finally, actually alone.

So she stays up. Not because there's something worth watching. Just because this hour feels like hers in a way the rest of the day didn't.

She gets it. The night feels like the only space that hasn't been claimed yet. But she's learning something slowly: the tired version of herself who gets this time isn't really getting much from it.

The rested version of herself tomorrow gets something better — a morning that doesn't feel like punishment, a mind that can actually be present.

If you had 20 minutes tomorrow that were completely yours — not stolen from sleep, but actually given to yourself — when would they be?

title: The open tabs
body:
It's not one thing keeping Zara up. It's the accumulation. A message she half-wrote and didn't send. A task she keeps forgetting. Something someone said that she's still turning over. A deadline that isn't tomorrow but feels like it is.

She opens her notes app and types three words for each thing. Not to solve them. Just to put them somewhere outside her head.

'Tomorrow problems,' she labels the note.

It sounds simple. It kind of works anyway. The thoughts are still there, but they're somewhere else now, waiting. They don't need her tonight.

She puts the phone down. Her mind is quieter than it was a minute ago — not empty, just less crowded.

What's the one thought that's been keeping you here tonight?

title: Nothing new
body:
Selin has checked the same three apps four times in the last ten minutes. Nothing has changed. Nobody has posted anything she hasn't seen. There are no new messages.

She checks again anyway.

She recognises this loop. It's not really about information anymore — it's just something for her hands to do, somewhere to aim her attention while she avoids the transition to sleep.

She puts the phone face-down. If something important happens tonight, she will find out in the morning. She has never once woken up to genuinely important news that couldn't have waited.

The room settles around her. The quiet is different when you stop reaching through it. She breathes out slowly, and for the first time tonight, she isn't waiting for anything.

What are you hoping to find when you check one more time?

title: Wired and hollow
body:
Yusuf is tired in the specific way that makes sleep feel far away. Not sleepy-tired — wired-tired. The kind where your eyes sting but your brain won't stop.

He knows that more screen time is making it worse. He knows the stimulation is keeping him in exactly this state. He knows all of this, and he's still scrolling.

He puts the phone down and just lies there. It feels strange for about thirty seconds — like there should be something to do with his hands, something to look at. Then it doesn't feel strange anymore. His breathing slows without him trying.

The sleepy feeling that was hiding underneath the wired feeling starts to surface. It always does. He always forgets that it will.

When did you last let yourself just be still — not trying to sleep, just lying in the dark doing nothing at all?

title: Two mornings
body:
Amara has lived both versions of tomorrow morning many times.

In one, she wakes up before the alarm has fully finished, slow and resentful, bargaining for five more minutes that turn into twenty. She's behind before the day has started. Everything feels like effort.

In the other, she wakes up tired — still tired, because she's always a little tired — but not resentful. She has time. The morning doesn't feel like an obstacle course.

The difference between these two mornings is mostly what she does in the next ten minutes.

She doesn't need a perfect sleep. She doesn't need to fall asleep immediately. She just needs to stop adding to the deficit right now.

She puts the phone down and pulls the duvet up a little. Small. Easy. Done.

Which version of tomorrow do you want?

title: Empty the jar
body:
Before you put the phone down, take sixty seconds to do one thing.

Open your notes. Type out everything that's currently sitting in your head — tasks, worries, half-formed thoughts, things you're afraid you'll forget. Don't organise them. Don't prioritise them. Just get them out of your head and into the note.

Research from Baylor University found that people who wrote a specific to-do list before bed fell asleep significantly faster than those who didn't. The brain isn't good at letting go of unfinished things — but it's surprisingly willing to let go of them once they're written down somewhere it trusts.

Your head isn't a safe place to store tomorrow's problems. Your notes app is.

Take sixty seconds. Write it all down. Then put the phone on charge and let your brain off the hook for tonight.

What's one thing you can move from your head onto the page right now?

title: Tomorrow's list
body:
Here's something worth trying tonight.

Open your notes and spend just three minutes writing down the specific tasks you need to do tomorrow — not vague intentions, but actual named things. 'Email Dr. Ahmed about the extension.' 'Start introduction for the essay.' 'Message back about Friday.'

The more specific, the better. Vague tasks stay alive in your head because your brain keeps trying to work out what they actually mean. Specific tasks can be put down, because your brain knows exactly what to pick up in the morning.

This isn't about being productive. It's about giving your brain permission to stop working for the night. Once the list exists outside your head, the mental effort of holding onto everything is no longer yours to carry.

Three minutes. Then phone on charge, lights off, and the rest belongs to tomorrow.

What are the two or three most specific things you need to remember for tomorrow?

title: The worry and what's within reach
body:
If there's something weighing on you tonight — a decision, a conversation, a result you're waiting for — try this.

Write one sentence describing what you're worried about. Just one.

Then write the answer to two questions: How likely is it, really? And what part of it is actually within your control?

Most worries at midnight feel total and inevitable. They rarely are. And the parts that aren't within your control aren't yours to solve tonight — or ever, really.

The parts that are within your control have an action attached to them. Write that action down. Give it a time tomorrow. Now it's scheduled, not floating.

You're not solving the problem tonight. You're just finding its edges — and discovering that it has them.

What's the one worry that most needs a container tonight?

title: Three small things
body:
Before you sleep, try to find three small things from today that were okay.

Not good, necessarily. Not achievements. Just things that happened today that were okay, or warm, or that you're quietly glad about.

A coffee that tasted right. A message from someone you like. A moment when something clicked. A ten-minute walk where you weren't thinking about anything in particular. The feeling of getting into bed when you're genuinely tired.

Research consistently shows that actively noticing small positive moments — not forcing gratitude, just noticing — can shift the emotional tone of the day before sleep. It doesn't erase difficulty. It just adds weight to the other side of the scale.

You don't need to write them down. You can just hold them for a moment before you close your eyes.

What are three small things from today that were quietly okay?

title: The winter bookshop
body:
Close your eyes and imagine walking into a small bookshop just as evening falls.

The door is heavy and wooden, and it closes softly behind you, muffling the cold outside. The shop is warm — almost too warm — with that specific warmth that comes from old paper and radiators that have been on all day.

The shelves go up to the ceiling. There's no particular order to the books; you just drift along them, trailing your fingers across spines, not looking for anything. The light is amber and low. Somewhere near the back there's a cat asleep on a chair.

You're not here for anything. You're just here.

Notice the quiet. The weight of the books around you. The way your breathing has slowed without you asking it to. The cold outside feels far away. Everything feels slightly far away.

You could stay here a while. There's no reason to rush.

title: The slow train north
body:
Imagine you're on a night train, sitting by the window in a warm carriage.

Outside, the landscape is dark — occasional lights from farmhouses, the outline of hills against a slightly lighter sky. The train moves at a gentle, unhurried pace, the sound of it rhythmic and low, like a slow exhale repeated over and over.

You have nowhere to be when you arrive. No one is expecting you. The journey itself is the point.

Your seat is soft. There's a blanket folded on the armrest that you pull across your lap. The window is slightly cold when you lean your head against it, but it's the right kind of cold — it makes the warmth of everything else more felt.

The train rocks you almost imperceptibly. The lights of a small town slide past and disappear. The darkness returns.

You watch it go. You let the motion carry you.

title: The lake at dusk
body:
Imagine lying in a wooden canoe on a still lake, just as the light begins to go.

The water barely moves. When it does, it makes a small, soft sound against the sides of the boat — a sound so regular it becomes part of the quiet rather than interrupting it.

Above you, the sky is the deep blue of early evening, not quite dark. One or two stars have appeared. The air is cool and clean and smells of water and pine.

Your hands rest at your sides. The canoe holds your weight completely — you don't have to do anything to stay afloat. You're just there, held, on the surface of a very still lake, while the world slowly dims around you.

You can feel the tension leaving your shoulders, your jaw, your hands. Your body is heavier than you realised.

You're not going anywhere. You're just here, floating.

title: The quiet garden
body:
Imagine a small walled garden in the early morning, just after sunrise, when the air is still cool and everything is slightly damp.

There is no one else here. You move slowly along the path — it's made of old flat stones, unevenly spaced, and you step carefully from one to the next. On either side, plants grow in the unhurried way that plants grow: spilling over the edges of beds, resting against the wall, leaning slightly toward the light.

You're not maintaining the garden. You're just moving through it.

The sound is almost nothing — a bird somewhere distant, the faint creak of a wooden gate in a soft breeze. Your footsteps on the path. Your breathing.

You stop and look at something growing close to the ground. You don't know what it is. It doesn't matter. You just look at it for a moment, and then you move on.

The garden has no edges you need to reach.

title: The rule Kai made
body:
Kai made a rule for himself: phone on charge at 11. He breaks it most nights — not because he forgets, but because 11pm arrives and there's always something that makes it feel too early.

Tonight he notices the time at 11:03 and does it anyway. Not because he suddenly has more willpower. Just because he's finally more tired of being tired than he is of the rule.

He puts the phone across the room. Gets into bed. Stares at the ceiling for a few minutes.

He doesn't feel virtuous about it. He just feels like someone who made one small choice that his future self might appreciate.

Is there one rule you could set for yourself — small enough to actually keep — that would make it easier to wind down? Not a perfect rule. Just one that works better than none.

title: The episode that wasn't worth it
body:
Omar watched one more episode. It was fine. Not bad, not great — the kind of fine that doesn't justify the hour it took, or the heavy feeling behind his eyes now, or the fact that it's somehow 1:15am.

He's not angry at himself. He's just noting it, the way you note something that keeps happening so you can understand it better.

The thing about one more episode is that it's never really about the episode. It's about the gap between wanting to stop and knowing how to stop. The episode is just what fills that gap.

He turns the screen off. The room is darker than he expected. He lies down.

He doesn't promise himself he'll never do it again. He just lets tonight be over.

If you're honest with yourself — was what you were watching actually worth the cost of tomorrow morning?

title: Just the smallest thing
body:
Chloe is not going to overhaul her sleep tonight. She's not going to start a new routine, download a meditation app, or make any promises to herself.

She's just going to do the smallest possible thing.

Phone on charge. That's it. Across the room, on charge, face-down. Thirty seconds of effort.

She's learned that big intentions at midnight don't survive contact with tomorrow. But small actions sometimes do. Putting the phone across the room is a thing she can actually do, right now, without willpower or motivation or feeling ready.

She does it.

The room is quiet. Her body is heavier than she realised. She didn't notice how tired she was until she stopped moving toward the next thing and let herself arrive at the end of the day.

What's the smallest possible action you could take right now — not to fix your sleep, just to take one step toward it?
